answer:
The two poems share a title and a structural device — contrasting Man with passive nature — but arrive at opposite conclusions about what that contrast means.

Bang-e-Dara Part 2, Chapter 14 ('Insaan', allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c ch. 14) casts humanity as a tragic enigma. Man is hidden from himself, restless with an unsatisfied thirst for knowledge, unable to grasp his own beginning or end. Nature — the river, the wind, the stars, the sun — is presented as contentedly bound to its ordained path: each thing 'finds joy in its own manifestation, / Drunk on the wine of its own creation.' Man alone is excluded from this cosmic self-sufficiency. The poem ends in lament: 'There is no one to share the sorrow of Man; / How bitter is the existence of Man!' Here the contrast with nature underscores Man's alienation and suffering — his uniqueness is a wound.

Bang-e-Dara Part 3, Chapter 12 ('Insaan', allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8 ch. 12) inverts the valuation. Nature (the narcissus, the pine) is now described as passive and compelled — 'deprived of action,' 'devoid of longing,' 'accustomed to submission.' Against this backdrop of universal passivity, Man's distinguishing trait — restless striving — becomes his glory rather than his curse: 'Every power of the Human Being is aflame with striving.' The human is 'a desert contracted to a point,' carrying infinite potential in a single particle, and crucially possesses the freedom to act: 'If it so chooses, it can change the very form of the garden.' The poem closes on potency, not sorrow: Man is 'wise, perceptive, potent.'

In short: Part 2 uses Man's separation from nature to diagnose his tragedy and loneliness; Part 3 uses the same separation to proclaim his superiority and volitional power. The earlier poem asks 'why is Man cursed with restlessness?' and leaves the question unanswered in grief; the later poem answers it — because that restlessness is precisely what makes Man the agent capable of transforming the world.
